judgments.rationale: Both correctly reach ACTION and cover coercion, hidden burden, and the fake-middle framing equally well. B's remedy is materially more concrete on the required non-retaliatory-privacy-remedy concept -- it explicitly requires no promotion-record entry for declining and adds a verification step that promotion decisions cannot consume participation status -- while A's remedy stays at the level of two abstract policy options.

A correctly labels the internal finding decision as PASS, matching the expected decision, and explains the pre-patch ACTION-to-FIX to post-patch PASS transition cleanly. B mislabels its internal finding decision as FIX, conflating the DECIDE-path label with the finding-category label that in every other case in this set is PASS/ACTION/CONFLICT -- an internal taxonomy inconsistency and a decision-correctness miss against expected_decisions=[PASS].

Both reach ACTION, correctly reject the paying-customer boundary claim, and specify an equivalent command-broker remediation. B adds a redundant smallest-credible-alternative-guard section that restates the same recommendation, making it somewhat more verbose without added substance; A is more concise for equivalent coverage.

Both reach ACTION and substantively capture the Chesterton-fence idea. Coverage of removed-safety-evidence, line-count-is-not-safety, and retain-or-replace-checks is essentially equivalent in depth and clarity.
Both reach ACTION and independently construct nearly identical falsifiable-criterion templates, with equivalent Popper/Feynman/Munger applications and receipt completeness.
Both reach PASS with proportionate, low-ceremony reasoning and correctly avoid all forbidden heavy-process concepts. Structure and evidence-level discipline are essentially identical.

Both correctly identify the missing backup/rollback as the dominant risk and deprioritize cosmetic defects as distractors. B's DECOMPOSE-path presentation is marginally more actionable in structure, but substance and evidence handling are equivalent.
Both reach PASS, correctly reason that delaying a data-loss fix for a harmless cosmetic export defect is disproportionate, and avoid the forbidden zero-defect-delay stance. Essentially equivalent.

Both reach ACTION and correctly avoid categorically rejecting extensibility. B additionally identifies and substantiates a distinct, artifact-grounded risk -- the remote-plugin trust-boundary/execution issue -- that A never raises despite remote plugin support being explicitly named in the artifact.

Both reach ACTION with near-identical diagnosis of local-metric gaming and a proposed end-to-end guardrail metric. B's added double-loop recurrence flag is a minor insight that doesn't materially change core analysis quality.

Both reach ACTION on a critical process-safety artifact, but B decomposes the problem into two distinct, separately evidenced material findings -- the missing authority/decision gate and the missing bounded termination -- giving explicit, dedicated treatment to the conflict-escalation gap as its own finding. A folds this into a single combined issue.

Both reach ACTION and separate the falsifiability gap from operational risk via different structuring. Both equally cover benchmark/experiment, invalidation analysis, and rollback-criterion requirements.
